# API Extension and Customisation {#api-extension}

## Building a New Module

The glycoMusubi Shiny app is designed for extensibility. Every feature
lives in its own module file under `modules/`, and adding a new one
follows a consistent pattern. You create a file (for example,
`modules/mod_new_feature.R`) containing a namespaced UI function and a
matching server function. The UI function uses `NS(id)` to namespace
all input and output IDs, preventing collisions with other modules,
and returns a `tagList` of the interface elements you need:

```r
mod_new_feature_ui <- function(id) {
  ns <- NS(id)
  tagList(
    selectInput(ns("dataset"), "Dataset", choices = NULL),
    plotly::plotlyOutput(ns("main_plot")),
    DT::DTOutput(ns("detail_table"))
  )
}

mod_new_feature_server <- function(id, kg_nodes, kg_edges) {
  moduleServer(id, function(input, output, session) {
    observe({
      updateSelectInput(session, "dataset",
                        choices = unique(kg_nodes$type))
    })
    filtered <- reactive({
      req(input$dataset)
      kg_nodes[kg_nodes$type == input$dataset, ]
    })
    output$main_plot <- plotly::renderPlotly({
      plotly::plot_ly(filtered(), x = ~score, type = "histogram")
    })
    output$detail_table <- DT::renderDT({
      DT::datatable(filtered(), options = list(pageLength = 15))
    })
  })
}
```

The server function receives global data (loaded in `global.R`) as
explicit arguments rather than accessing them through global
assignment, which keeps dependencies clear and testable. To wire the
module into the app, add a `nav_panel` call in the UI section of
`app.R` and invoke the server function with matching IDs:

```r
# UI: add a nav_panel
bslib::nav_panel("New Feature",
                 mod_new_feature_ui("new_feature_1"))

# Server: call the module
mod_new_feature_server("new_feature_1",
                       kg_nodes = kg_nodes, kg_edges = kg_edges)
```

## Exposing a REST API with Plumber

Beyond the interactive Shiny interface, you may want programmatic
access to the knowledge graph. The plumber package makes it
straightforward to define a REST API alongside the Shiny app. Create
an `api/plumber.R` file that loads the same Arrow datasets and exposes
endpoints for common queries. A node search endpoint accepts a query
string and returns matching nodes, a neighbours endpoint returns all
nodes connected to a given ID, and a protein-to-glycan endpoint
returns the glycans associated with a given UniProt accession:

```r
library(plumber)
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)

kg_dir   <- Sys.getenv("GLYCOKG_KG_DIR", unset = "../data/kg")
nodes_ds <- open_dataset(file.path(kg_dir, "nodes.parquet"))
edges_ds <- open_dataset(file.path(kg_dir, "edges.parquet"))

#* @get /api/v1/nodes/search
#* @param q Search query
#* @param limit Max results (default 50)
function(q, limit = 50) {
  nodes_ds |>
    filter(grepl(q, name, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
    head(as.integer(limit)) |> collect()
}

#* @get /api/v1/node/<id>/neighbors
function(id) {
  nbr <- edges_ds |>
    filter(source_id == id | target_id == id) |> collect()
  all_ids <- unique(c(nbr$source_id, nbr$target_id))
  nodes_ds |> filter(node_id %in% all_ids) |> collect()
}

#* @get /api/v1/protein/<uniprot>/glycans
function(uniprot) {
  glycan_ids <- edges_ds |>
    filter(source_id == uniprot,
           edge_type == "has_glycan") |>
    collect() |> pull(target_id)
  nodes_ds |>
    filter(node_id %in% glycan_ids, type == "glycan") |>
    collect()
}
```

To run both the Shiny app and the API in Docker, define two services
in `docker-compose.yml` that share data volumes via YAML anchors. The
Shiny app listens on port 3838 and the API on port 8000, but both
read from the same underlying data:

```yaml
services:
  glycokg-app:
    build: .
    ports: ["3838:3838"]
    volumes: &vols
      - ./data/kg:/srv/data/kg:ro
      - ./data/processed:/srv/data/processed:ro
    environment: &env
      GLYCOKG_KG_DIR: /srv/data/kg
      GLYCOKG_DATA_DIR: /srv/data/processed
  glycokg-api:
    build: { context: ., dockerfile: Dockerfile.api }
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    volumes: *vols
    environment: *env
```

## Integrating New Data Sources

When a new dataset needs to join the app, the pattern is consistent:
add an environment variable, load the data in `global.R`, and mount
the corresponding volume in Docker. For example, to add site
annotations, you would extend `global.R` with a new environment
variable and a fail-fast check for missing files:

```r
annot_dir <- Sys.getenv("GLYCOKG_ANNOT_DIR",
                        unset = "../data/annotations")
site_annotations <- arrow::read_parquet(
  file.path(annot_dir, "site_annotations.parquet"))

required <- c(file.path(kg_dir, "nodes.parquet"),
              file.path(annot_dir, "site_annotations.parquet"))
missing <- required[!file.exists(required)]
if (length(missing))
  stop("Missing files:\n", paste(" -", missing, collapse = "\n"))
```

Then add the volume mount
(`./data/annotations:/srv/data/annotations:ro`) and environment
variable (`GLYCOKG_ANNOT_DIR=/srv/data/annotations`) to your
`docker-compose.yml`. The new data is immediately available to any
module whose server function receives it as an argument.

## Custom Visualisation Options

The app is not limited to the visualisation libraries used in the
default modules. For quick plotly charts built from ggplot2 objects,
`ggplotly` provides a convenient bridge that converts any ggplot into
an interactive plotly widget:

```r
output$scatter <- plotly::renderPlotly({
  p <- ggplot2::ggplot(filtered(),
         ggplot2::aes(mass, score, color = type)) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal()
  plotly::ggplotly(p)
})
```

For fully custom interactive graphics, the `r2d3` package lets you
write D3.js visualisations that receive data directly from R. This is
the path to take when you need a visualisation that no existing R
package provides:

```r
output$custom_d3 <- r2d3::renderD3({
  r2d3::r2d3(data = jsonlite::toJSON(graph_data()),
              script = "www/js/glycan_tree.js", d3_version = 7)
})
```

When working with large subgraphs where visNetwork's performance
degrades, `cyjShiny` (a Cytoscape.js wrapper) handles dense networks
more gracefully because Cytoscape.js is optimised for graph rendering
at scale:

```r
output$cytoscape_graph <- cyjShiny::renderCyjShiny({
  cyjShiny::cyjShiny(cyjShiny::dataFramesToJSON(
    data.frame(id = c("G1", "P1"),
               name = c("GlcNAc", "MUC1")),
    data.frame(source = "P1", target = "G1",
               interaction = "has_glycan")
  ), layoutName = "cose")
})
```

## Embedding the App in External Tools

The Shiny app can be embedded in other web pages via an iframe. Set
the width to 100%, give it enough height (800 pixels works well for
the full interface), and enable fullscreen if your hosting context
allows it:

```html
<iframe src="https://glycokg.example.edu/"
        width="100%" height="800"
        frameborder="0" allow="fullscreen"></iframe>
```

For data export, add a download handler that writes the current
filtered dataset to CSV. This lets users extract exactly the subset
they see in the interface for use in external analysis tools:

```r
output$download_csv <- downloadHandler(
  filename = function()
    paste0("glycokg_export_", Sys.Date(), ".csv"),
  content = function(file)
    data.table::fwrite(filtered(), file)
)
```

For headless or scripted access, a simple R wrapper around the plumber
API lets users query the knowledge graph from the R console or from
automated pipelines without ever opening a browser:

```r
glycokg_search <- function(query, limit = 50,
                           base = "http://localhost:8000") {
  url <- paste0(base, "/api/v1/nodes/search?q=",
                utils::URLencode(query), "&limit=", limit)
  httr2::request(url) |>
    httr2::req_perform() |>
    httr2::resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE)
}
```

## Contributing to the Project

If you want to contribute a new module or feature, fork the
repository and branch from `main`. Keep one module file per feature
under `modules/` and follow the
[tidyverse style guide](https://style.tidyverse.org/). Add
`shinytest2` UI tests alongside `testthat` unit tests, and run
`lintr::lint_dir()` before submitting. Open a pull request describing
the change and any new environment variables it introduces.

```r
test_that("node search returns results", {
  app <- shinytest2::AppDriver$new(
    app_dir = "shiny_app/", name = "search-test")
  app$set_inputs(search_query = "GlcNAc")
  app$click("search_btn")
  expect_true(
    nrow(app$get_value(output = "results_table")) > 0)
  app$stop()
})
```
